Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty loose. I'd try to set aside time on weekends mostly, maybe a couple hours here and there during the week. With five online courses going at once, I found myself just trying to keep my head above water rather than having any strict schedule. I'd usually read through materials and take notes in my notebook since that's how I learn best. Some weeks I'd manage twelve solid hours, other weeks it was scattered and unfocused.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines. I'd coast along doing minimal work, then suddenly realize something was due and put in a concentrated effort. It wasn't the smartest approach, but with my motivation being what it was, I just couldn't maintain consistency. When multiple assignments lined up across my courses, everything got chaotic and I'd have to prioritize which ones mattered most.
Q3: Usually it was checking if something was due soon or if there were any announcements I'd missed. Sometimes I'd log in because I got a notification from the peer support group I was connected with. I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse around—there had to be a specific reason, like needing to submit something or check my grades.
Q4: For a while in the middle of the semester, I got into a rhythm of logging in every few days to check discussion posts. The peer support connection helped with that because I felt some accountability to respond to people. But honestly, it never became truly automatic for me. Once I started feeling down about my progress, even that routine fell apart.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me since I'm a read-write learner. I'd download PDFs and articles, then go through them with a highlighter and notebook. The discussion forums were also helpful when I actually engaged with them—seeing how other students interpreted things gave me different perspectives. The peer interactions pushed me to articulate my own understanding better.
Q6: I often skipped the longer video lectures or only watched portions of them. Over 200 minutes of video sounds like a lot, but I'd often have them playing while doing other things, not fully absorbing the content. I found reading the same material in text form much more efficient for my learning style. Some of the optional practice quizzes I also ignored because they felt like extra work without direct grade impact.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam, or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was confused about. When I could see how spending twenty minutes on something would pay off in my assignment score, I'd do it. But if something felt like busywork or redundant, my motivation just wasn't there to engage with it properly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford emotionally. Required work already felt overwhelming with my low energy levels, so optional stuff got pushed aside almost automatically. There was always this guilt about not doing the extras, but I had to be realistic about what I could handle. Required activities at least had the pressure of consequences, which sometimes was the only thing getting me to act.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download readings and work through them in my room without any internet connection. I have a whole notebook filled with summaries and notes that the LMS would never know about. Sometimes I'd study from textbooks I owned or materials I'd found elsewhere online that explained concepts better than the course materials.
Q10: There were many nights where I sat with printed materials or my notebook, working through problems or re-reading concepts for hours. None of that showed up anywhere digitally. I also had study sessions with a couple of peers where we'd work through difficult topics together in person—real learning was happening, but the LMS only saw silence from my account during those times.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach. I'd access a reading or download a file once, then spend days working through it offline. My actual engagement with that material was much deeper than a single download timestamp would suggest. I preferred having physical copies I could mark up and return to without needing to log in again.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard for me. I was feeling sad and overwhelmed, and my resilience just wasn't there to push through. Missing classes—my attendance dropped to around half—made me feel even more disconnected. The peer support helped somewhat, but there were weeks where even opening the LMS felt like too much effort.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or anxious, I'd log in, stare at the dashboard, and then close it without doing anything productive. Competing responsibilities from my other courses meant I was constantly triaging what absolutely needed attention. The mental load of managing five online courses while not feeling great emotionally meant the LMS often got superficial engagement from me.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd scroll through materials or have videos playing, but my mind was elsewhere—worrying about other deadlines or just feeling flat and unmotivated. The system probably registered me as 'active' during those times, but I wasn't really absorbing anything. It was going through the motions without genuine cognitive engagement.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts and some readings constantly, even knowing they mattered for participation grades. The anxiety of not being prepared made me avoid things rather than face them. It's a frustrating cycle—the more I avoided, the more anxious I got, which made me avoid more. Important things got delayed until the last possible moment or sometimes missed entirely.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the engine driving all my LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active—downloading materials, checking requirements, submitting work. Without that external pressure, I'd drift. My engagement graph probably looked like a series of spikes around due dates rather than steady participation.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did affect me, though not always positively. Good feedback motivated me briefly, but critical feedback sometimes made me want to disengage because it confirmed my fears about not being good enough. I tried to use feedback constructively, but with my mood being low, it was hard not to take criticism personally and let it demotivate me.
Q18: The course structure with weekly modules was helpful in theory, but I often fell behind and then felt lost trying to catch up. Having clear deadlines helped me prioritize, but the flexibility of online learning was a double-edged sword for someone with my motivation levels. More rigid structure might have actually helped me stay on track better.
Q19: The reading-heavy format suited my learning style, which was good. But the lack of immediate accountability—no one noticing if I missed a week—made it easy to slip. The peer support intervention helped because it added a human element, people who might notice if I disappeared. Without that, I think I would have disengaged even more.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is searching for understanding but struggling to find it. They might be clicking through materials repeatedly, trying different resources, but still not grasping things well enough to complete assignments confidently. Or they could be dealing with anxiety that makes them check the LMS obsessively without being able to focus on actual work.
Q21: That definitely resonates with my experience. I performed reasonably well—passed my exams—despite not being highly active in the LMS because I did substantial work offline. My reading and note-taking happened away from the platform. Someone looking at my logs might think I wasn't trying, but I was just learning in ways that didn't register digitally.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, submission times. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if I was deeply focused or mindlessly scrolling. They miss offline study, emotional state, and whether the learning actually stuck. The numbers tell a story, but it's an incomplete one.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, asking questions in my head, connecting ideas to other things I know. Real engagement is when I lose track of time because I'm genuinely interested. Lately, I've struggled to feel that—I've been going through motions more than truly engaging.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally throughout this course. It wouldn't capture the sadness that made logging in feel exhausting, or the anxiety that made me avoid things I knew were important. It also wouldn't show the genuine effort I put in offline, or how the peer support connections actually helped me more than any course material did.
